title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Hollywood development is a portfolio problem: find a concept with broad appeal, attach credible talent, and reduce uncertainty by learning from prior successes. This report uses IMDb’s non-commercial datasets to identify durable genres, successful personnel, and a classic title suitable for a contemporary remake.


Show code
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
pkgs <- c("tidyverse", "scales", "DT", "knitr", "stringr")
missing_pkgs <- pkgs[!vapply(pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) install.packages(missing_pkgs)

library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(DT)
library(knitr)
library(stringr)

dir.create("data/mp02", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache.lazy = FALSE)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 1: Column Type Correction


Show code
NAME_BASICS <- NAME_BASICS |>
  mutate(birthYear = as.numeric(birthYear), deathYear = as.numeric(deathYear))

TITLE_BASICS <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  mutate(
    isAdult = as.logical(as.integer(isAdult)),
    startYear = as.numeric(startYear),
    endYear = as.numeric(endYear),
    runtimeMinutes = as.numeric(runtimeMinutes)
  )

TITLE_EPISODES <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  mutate(seasonNumber = as.numeric(seasonNumber), episodeNumber = as.numeric(episodeNumber))

TITLE_RATINGS <- TITLE_RATINGS |>
  mutate(averageRating = as.numeric(averageRating), numVotes = as.numeric(numVotes))

TITLE_PRINCIPALS <- TITLE_PRINCIPALS |>
  mutate(ordering = as.numeric(ordering))

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 2: Instructor-Provided Questions


Show code
title_counts <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(titleType %in% c("movie", "tvSeries", "tvEpisode")) |>
  count(titleType, name = "n") |>
  arrange(desc(n))

oldest_living <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(!is.na(birthYear), is.na(deathYear)) |>
  arrange(birthYear) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(primaryName, birthYear, primaryProfession, knownForTitles)

perfect_episode <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  filter(titleType == "tvEpisode", averageRating == 10, numVotes >= 200000) |>
  left_join(TITLE_EPISODES, join_by(tconst)) |>
  left_join(
    TITLE_BASICS |> select(parentTconst = tconst, seriesTitle = primaryTitle, seriesStart = startYear),
    join_by(parentTconst)
  ) |>
  select(primaryTitle, seriesTitle, seasonNumber, episodeNumber, averageRating, numVotes)

mark_hamill_known_for <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryName == "Mark Hamill") |>
  separate_longer_delim(knownForTitles, ",") |>
  left_join(TITLE_BASICS, join_by(knownForTitles == tconst)) |>
  select(primaryName, primaryTitle, titleType, startYear, genres)

best_series_12_plus <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  group_by(parentTconst) |>
  summarize(
    episodes = n(),
    avg_episode_rating = weighted.mean(averageRating, numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_votes = sum(numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(episodes > 12) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_BASICS, join_by(parentTconst == tconst)) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_episode_rating), desc(total_votes)) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, episodes, avg_episode_rating, total_votes) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

happy_days <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryTitle == "Happy Days", titleType == "tvSeries", startYear == 1974) |>
  slice_head(n = 1)

happy_days_by_season <- TITLE_EPISODES |>
  filter(parentTconst == happy_days$tconst[1]) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  group_by(seasonNumber) |>
  summarize(
    episodes = n(),
    average_rating = weighted.mean(averageRating, numVotes, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(seasonNumber))

  geom_line(color = "#2c7fb8", linewidth = 1) +

    x = "Season",

  theme_minimal()









The season trend provides the empirical version of the “jump the shark” claim. If later seasons sit below the early seasons, the idiom matches the crowd ratings; if the line is flatter, the phrase is more culturally memorable than statistically dramatic.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #02: The Business of Show Business
text:
Task 3: Custom Success Metric
I define success as a blend of quality and audience reach:
success_score = averageRating * log10(numVotes + 1)
This keeps the rating scale meaningful while preventing vote count alone from dominating.


Show code
TITLE_RATINGS <- TITLE_RATINGS |>
  mutate(success_score = averageRating * log10(numVotes + 1))

movie_success <- TITLE_BASICS |>
  filter(titleType == "movie", !is.na(startYear), !isAdult) |>
  inner_join(TITLE_RATINGS, join_by(tconst)) |>
  mutate(decade = floor(startYear / 10) * 10)

top_success_movies <- movie_success |>
  slice_max(success_score, n = 10) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

popular_low_quality <- movie_success |>
  filter(numVotes >= quantile(numVotes, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  slice_min(success_score, n = 5) |>
  select(primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

prestige_person <- "Christopher Nolan"
prestige_projects <- NAME_BASICS |>
  filter(primaryName == prestige_person) |>
  separate_longer_delim(knownForTitles, ",") |>
  left_join(movie_success, join_by(knownForTitles == tconst)) |>
  arrange(desc(success_score)) |>
  select(primaryName, primaryTitle, startYear, genres, averageRating, numVotes, success_score)

recent_solid_check <- movie_success |>
  filter(startYear >= 2010, numVotes >= 100000) |>
  summarize(
    median_success = median(success_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75_success = quantile(success_score, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    p90_success = quantile(success_score, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

success_threshold <- quantile(movie_success$success_score, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)

This report studies county-level shifts from the 2020 to 2024 U.S. presidential elections. The pipeline follows the assignment constraint: county results are scraped from Wikipedia rather than imported from an election package, and county shapes come from the U.S. Census Bureau.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
Task 5: County Shift Map
The map uses county centroids and arrows. Rightward shifts point east; leftward shifts point west. Alaska and Hawaii are shifted and rescaled into a more reasonable position so the map keeps all states while remaining legible.


Show code
state_base <- map_data("state")

shift_shapes <- county_shapes |>
  left_join(election_shift, join_by(county_fips)) |>
  filter(!is.na(trump_share_shift))

centroids <- shift_shapes |>
  st_transform(4326) |>
  st_point_on_surface() |>
  mutate(
    x = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 1],
    y = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 2],
    x = case_when(
      STATEFP == "02" ~ x * 0.35 - 95,
      STATEFP == "15" ~ x + 52,
      TRUE ~ x
    ),
    y = case_when(
      STATEFP == "02" ~ y * 0.35 + 16,
      STATEFP == "15" ~ y + 5,
      TRUE ~ y
    ),
    xend = x + trump_share_shift * 4,
    yend = y,
    direction = if_else(trump_share_shift >= 0, "Rightward", "Leftward")
  ) |>
  st_drop_geometry()




Show code
par(mar = c(0, 0, 3, 0))
map("state", fill = TRUE, col = "grey96", border = "white", lwd = 0.2)
segment_colors <- if_else(centroids$direction == "Rightward", "#d95f0e99", "#2c7fb899")
segments(
  centroids$x,
  centroids$y,
  centroids$xend,
  centroids$yend,
  col = segment_colors,
  lwd = 0.5
)
title("County-Level Presidential Vote Shift, 2020 to 2024")
legend(
  "bottomleft",
  legend = c("Rightward", "Leftward"),
  col = c("#d95f0e", "#2c7fb8"),
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)

section: Task 6: Partisan Talking Points and Statistical Tests
text:
Task 6: Partisan Talking Points and Statistical Tests
For this section I adopt the Democratic persona suggested by the assignment: Trump won, but the victory can be framed as narrower than the county map makes it appear. I found this framing exercise genuinely interesting — the same underlying numbers support very different narratives depending on whether you weight by land area, county count, or actual votes cast.


Show code
weighted_shift <- with(election_shift, weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE))
median_shift <- median(election_shift$trump_share_shift, na.rm = TRUE)

set.seed(9750)
n_sims <- 200

bootstrap_median_shift <- replicate(
  n_sims,
  median(sample(election_shift$trump_share_shift, replace = TRUE), na.rm = TRUE)
)

bootstrap_weighted_shift <- replicate(
  n_sims,
  {
    boot_index <- sample.int(nrow(election_shift), nrow(election_shift), replace = TRUE)
    weighted.mean(
      election_shift$trump_share_shift[boot_index],
      election_shift$total_votes_2024[boot_index],
      na.rm = TRUE
    )
  }
)

median_shift_test <- tibble(
  test = "Bootstrap test: median county Trump shift is at least five points",
  estimate = median_shift,
  null_value = 0.05,
  p_value = mean(bootstrap_median_shift >= 0.05)
)

weighted_shift_test <- tibble(
  test = "Bootstrap test: vote-weighted Trump shift is at least five points",
  estimate = weighted_shift,
  null_value = 0.05,
  p_value = mean(bootstrap_weighted_shift >= 0.05)
)

shift_by_density <- election_shift |>
  mutate(density_group = ntile(voter_density_2020, 5)) |>
  group_by(density_group) |>
  summarize(
    counties = n(),
    median_density = median(voter_density_2020, na.rm = TRUE),
    weighted_trump_shift = weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_votes_2024 = sum(total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

state_shift <- election_shift |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarize(
    trump_shift = weighted.mean(trump_share_shift, total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    votes_2024 = sum(total_votes_2024, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




Show code
election_shift |>
  ggplot(aes(x = trump_share_shift)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#d95f0e", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent) +
  labs(
    title = "Most County Shifts Were Not a Five-Point Earthquake",
    x = "Trump share shift, 2020 to 2024",
    y = "Counties"
  )









Show code

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #04: Exploring Recent US Political Shifts
text:
Deliverable: Partisan Talking Points
Do not let a red county map do the talking for the country. Land does not vote; people do. The 2024 result moved toward Trump, but the evidence does not require the grandiose conclusion that America entered a new political era. The average county map is visually dramatic because thousands of low-population counties cover enormous space. When shifts are weighted by actual 2024 votes, the national movement is 1.6%, while the median county shift is 1.6%. That is a real change, but not a blank check for mythmaking.
The density analysis makes the point sharper. Dense counties, where more voters are packed into less geography, tell a quieter story than the wall-to-wall map of county arrows. The t-test against a five-point county shift also asks the question directly: was this really a sweeping five-point county earthquake? The answer is in the table above, and it is more modest than the loudest post-election narrative.
So the Democratic argument is this: Trump won the rules of the contest, but the visual drama overstates the mandate. The country shifted, yet unevenly. A disciplined opposition should study where turnout fell, where persuasion failed, and where small margins were allowed to become a national story. What struck me most while doing this analysis is how much the county-shift map — which looks overwhelming in red — changes character the moment you weight anything by population. That gap between geographic and demographic storytelling is the whole political argument in one picture.

section: Task 6: Farebox Recovery Among Major Systems
text:
Task 6: Farebox Recovery Among Major Systems
I define a major system as any agency-mode pair with at least 400,000 annual unlinked passenger trips. This threshold removes tiny systems whose ratios can swing wildly from one accounting entry. I tried a few different cutoffs — 100k, 400k, 1M — and 400k felt like the right balance between including real transit systems and excluding the outlier demand-response operations that have almost no riders but very high per-trip costs.


Show code
major_systems <- USAGE_AND_FINANCIALS |>
  filter(UPT >= 400000, VRM > 0, Expenses > 0)

summary_questions <- bind_rows(
  major_systems |> slice_max(UPT, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Most UPT in 2022"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(farebox_recovery, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest farebox recovery"),
  major_systems |> slice_min(expense_per_upt, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Lowest expenses per UPT"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(fare_per_upt, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest total fares per UPT"),
  major_systems |> slice_min(expense_per_vrm, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Lowest expenses per VRM"),
  major_systems |> slice_max(fare_per_vrm, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> mutate(metric = "Highest total fares per VRM")
) |>
  select(metric, Agency, Mode, metro_area, UPT, VRM, `Total Fares`, Expenses, farebox_recovery, expense_per_upt, fare_per_upt, expense_per_vrm, fare_per_vrm)

summary_questions |>
  mutate(across(c(UPT, VRM, `Total Fares`, Expenses), comma)) |>
  mutate(across(contains("per_"), dollar)) |>
  mutate(farebox_recovery = percent(farebox_recovery, accuracy = 0.1)) |>

title: Mini-Project #03: Creating the Ultimate Playlist
text:
Music is more than just sound; it is data. In this project, we dive into the world of music analytics to understand what makes a song “popular,” “danceable,” or fit for a specific mood. By analyzing a dataset of song characteristics and a collection of user-generated playlists, we aim to uncover trends in listening habits.
Our final goal is to leverage these insights to curate the Ultimate Playlist—a data-driven selection of tracks designed to maximize listener engagement through sonic continuity and energy flow.

text:
Task 5: Visualizations
1. Correlation between Popularity and Playlist Appearances
We define “popularity” using the Spotify popularity metric and compare it to our calculated appearance count.


Code
ggplot(combined_data, aes(x = popularity, y = playlist_appearances)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Spotify Popularity Score (0-100)", 
       y = "Count of Playlist Appearances",
       title = "Do 'Popular' Songs Appear More Often?")








Figure 1: Correlation between Spotify Popularity Score and Playlist Appearances





Interpretation: As seen in the figure, the regression line is nearly flat, indicating a surprisingly weak correlation between Spotify’s global “popularity” score and the frequency of appearances in these specific user playlists.
2. In what year were the most popular songs released?
We define “Popular Songs” as those with a Spotify popularity score > 80.


Code
combined_data |>
  filter(popularity > 80) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Golden Era of Hits",
       subtitle = "Release years of songs with Popularity > 80",
       x = "Release Year", y = "Count of Songs")








Figure 2: Distribution of Release Years for High-Popularity Songs





Interpretation: The data skews heavily toward recent years, which is expected given Spotify’s user demographic and the platform’s recency bias.
3. In what year did danceability peak?


Code
combined_data |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarize(avg_danceability = mean(danceability, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = avg_danceability)) +
  geom_line(color = "purple", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "The Groove Evolution",
       subtitle = "Average Danceability by Release Year",
       x = "Year", y = "Average Danceability (0-1)")








Figure 3: Average Danceability over Time





Interpretation: Danceability has seen a steady upward trend since the 1970s, peaking in the modern era.
4. Which decade is most represented on user playlists?


Code
combined_data |>
  mutate(decade = floor(year / 10) * 10) |>
  count(decade, wt = playlist_appearances) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(decade), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "firebrick") +
  labs(title = "Nostalgia Factor",
       subtitle = "Total Playlist Appearances by Decade",
       x = "Decade", y = "Total Appearances")








Figure 4: Playlist Representation by Decade





5. Key Frequency (Polar Coordinates)


Code
key_labels <- c("C", "C#", "D", "D#", "E", "F", "F#", "G", "G#", "A", "A#", "B")

combined_data |>
  count(key) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(key, labels = key_labels), y = n, fill = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_polar() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(title = "The Key to Success",
       subtitle = "Frequency of Musical Keys in Dataset",
       x = "Key", y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())








Figure 5: Distribution of Musical Keys





6. Most Popular Track Lengths


Code
combined_data |>
  mutate(duration_min = duration_ms / 60000) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = duration_min)) +
  geom_density(fill = "cyan", alpha = 0.5) +
  xlim(0, 8) + 
  labs(title = "Short & Sweet vs. Epic Sagas",
       subtitle = "Density of Track Durations (Minutes)",
       x = "Duration (Minutes)", y = "Density")








Figure 6: Distribution of Track Durations





Interpretation: The density plot reveals a strong preference for tracks between 3 and 4 minutes long, the industry standard for radio-friendly pop music.
7. Custom Exploratory Plots
The instructions require us to pose and answer at least two additional exploratory questions.
7a. Mood Mapping: Energy vs. Valence
Are happier songs always more energetic?


Code
combined_data |>
  sample_n(min(1000, nrow(combined_data))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = valence, y = energy, color = factor(mode, labels = c("Minor", "Major")))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Mood Mapping",
       subtitle = "Energy vs. Valence colored by Mode",
       x = "Valence (Positivity)", y = "Energy", color = "Mode")








Figure 7: Energy vs. Valence by Mode





7b. Loudness Distribution
How loud are the songs in this dataset? Is there a standardization?


Code
combined_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = loudness)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "darkred", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Turn It Up!",
       subtitle = "Distribution of Loudness (dB)",
       x = "Loudness (dB)", y = "Count")








Figure 8: Distribution of Song Loudness





Interpretation: The distribution of loudness is left-skewed, peaking around -5 to -8 dB. This aligns with modern mastering standards in the streaming era (often referred to as the “Loudness Wars”).

title: Mini-Project #03: Creating the Ultimate Playlist
Mini-Project #03: Creating the Ultimate Playlist
text:
Task 7: Curate and Analyze
We select a final list of 12 songs: the anchor plus a mix of popular hits and “hidden gems” (low popularity).


Code
set.seed(123)  # For reproducibility

final_playlist <- bind_rows(
  anchor,
  candidates |> filter(!is_popular) |> slice_sample(n = min(6, sum(!candidates$is_popular))), 
  candidates |> filter(is_popular) |> slice_sample(n = min(5, sum(candidates$is_popular))) 
) |> 
  distinct(name, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Visualize Evolution
final_playlist |>
  mutate(track_order = row_number()) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(energy, valence, danceability), 
               names_to = "metric", 
               values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = track_order, y = value, color = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Playlist Flow Analysis",
       subtitle = "Evolution of Energy, Valence, and Danceability",
       x = "Track Position", y = "Score")

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
In this project, I investigate the claim that the US Electoral College systematically biases election results. I analyze historical congressional and presidential election data to determine how different allocation schemes would have altered the outcomes of past elections.
Understanding the Electoral College’s impact on democratic representation is crucial for informed civic discourse. While the system was designed to balance state and federal interests, critics argue it can produce outcomes that diverge from the national popular will. This analysis examines that claim through rigorous data analysis.


Quick Answer: Senator Warren’s claim is Mostly True

Confirmed: Electoral College has favored Republicans in 2 of last 6 elections (2000, 2016)
Confirmed: System can produce winners who lost the popular vote -️ Nuanced: Common reforms (like District+2) would make outcomes MORE erratic, not less -️ Nuanced: No current bias is permanent; historical patterns were different

Bottom Line: The Electoral College does undermine vote equality, but fixing it requires careful reform design.




Claim: “My view is that every vote matters and the way we can make that happen is that we can have national voting, and that means get rid of the Electoral College.”
— Senator Elizabeth Warren (CNN Town Hall, 2019)

This report will retrodict election results under various alternative allocation rules to evaluate the veracity of this claim. I will examine four different electoral systems:

Winner-Take-All (Current System): The actual historical results
District + 2 (Maine/Nebraska Model): Each congressional district awards 1 EC vote to its winner; state winner receives 2 additional votes
Proportional Allocation: EC votes distributed according to state popular vote percentages
National Popular Vote: Direct election by nationwide popular vote

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
Task 1: Historical Shapefiles (1976-2012)
I download congressional district shapefiles from the authoritative Lewis et al. dataset, which provides boundaries for all US Congresses from 1789 to 2012.


Show code
# Function to download Lewis et al. shapefiles
download_lewis_shapes <- function() {
  base_url <- "[http://cdmaps.polisci.ucla.edu/shp/districts](http://cdmaps.polisci.ucla.edu/shp/districts)"
  # 94th Congress (1976) to 112th Congress (2012)
  for (congress in 94:112) {
    fname <- paste0("districts", sprintf("%03d", congress), ".zip")
    local_path <- file.path("data", fname)
    
    if (!file.exists(local_path)) {
      full_url <- paste0(base_url, sprintf("%03d", congress), ".zip")
      message(paste("Downloading", fname))
      tryCatch(
        download.file(full_url, destfile = local_path, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE),
        error = function(e) message(paste("Failed to download:", full_url))
      )
      Sys.sleep(0.5) # Be polite to the server
    }
  }
  message("Lewis et al. shapefiles download complete")
}

# Execute download
download_lewis_shapes()

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
Task 2: Modern Shapefiles (2014-2022)
For elections after 2012, I use shapefiles from the US Census Bureau TIGER/Line database, which provides the most current and accurate district boundaries.


Show code
# Function to download Census shapefiles
download_census_shapes <- function() {
  years <- c(2014, 2016, 2018, 2020, 2022)
  congress_nums <- c(114, 115, 116, 117, 118)
  
  for (i in seq_along(years)) {
    yr <- years[i]
    cong <- congress_nums[i]
    fname <- paste0("tl_", yr, "_us_cd", cong, ".zip")
    local_path <- file.path("data", fname)
    
    if (!file.exists(local_path)) {
      url <- paste0("[https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER](https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER)", yr, "/CD/", fname)
      message(paste("Downloading", fname))
      tryCatch(
        download.file(url, destfile = local_path, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE),
        error = function(e) message(paste("Failed to download:", url))
      )
      Sys.sleep(0.5) # Be polite to the server
    }
  }
  message("Census shapefiles download complete")
}

# Execute download
download_census_shapes()

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
1. Shifts in Congressional Representation
The US Constitution mandates reapportionment of House seats every 10 years following the Census. This reflects population shifts across states and directly impacts Electoral College vote distribution (since EC votes = House seats + 2 Senate seats).


Show code
min_yr <- min(house_data$year, na.rm = TRUE)
max_yr <- max(house_data$year, na.rm = TRUE)

seats_start <- house_data %>% 
  filter(year == min_yr) %>% 
  distinct(state, district) %>% 
  count(state, name = "seats_start")

seats_end <- house_data %>% 
  filter(year == max_yr) %>% 
  distinct(state, district) %>% 
  count(state, name = "seats_end")

seat_diff <- seats_start %>% 
  full_join(seats_end, by = "state") %>% 
  mutate(
    seats_start = replace_na(seats_start, 0),
    seats_end = replace_na(seats_end, 0),
    diff = seats_end - seats_start
  ) %>% 
  arrange(desc(diff))

# Display tables
kable(
  head(seat_diff, 5), 
  caption = paste("States Gaining Most Seats", min_yr, "-", max_yr),
  col.names = c("State", "Seats (1976)", "Seats (2024)", "Change")
)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
2. NY Fusion Voting Analysis
New York State allows candidates to appear on multiple party “lines” on the ballot, with votes across all lines counted together. This unique system can affect election outcomes when minor party support proves decisive.


Show code
ny_fusion_check <- house_data %>%
  filter(state == "NEW YORK") %>%
  # Sum votes by candidate across all party lines
  group_by(year, district, candidate) %>%
  mutate(total_candidate_votes = sum(candidatevotes)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Look at major party lines only (simulating "no fusion")
  filter(party %in% c("DEMOCRAT", "REPUBLICAN")) %>%
  select(year, district, candidate, party, major_party_votes = candidatevotes, total_candidate_votes) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  # Compare within district
  group_by(year, district) %>%
  filter(n() >= 2) %>%
  arrange(year, district, desc(total_candidate_votes)) %>%
  mutate(rank_total = row_number()) %>%
  arrange(year, district, desc(major_party_votes)) %>%
  mutate(rank_major = row_number()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Find flips: winner by total votes different from winner by major party votes
  group_by(year, district) %>%
  filter(any(rank_total == 1 & rank_major != 1)) %>%
  arrange(year, district, rank_total)

if (nrow(ny_fusion_check) > 0) {
  kable(
    head(ny_fusion_check, 8), 
    caption = "New York Elections Decided by Fusion Voting",
    col.names = c("Year", "District", "Candidate", "Party", "Major Party Votes", "Total Votes", "Rank (Total)", "Rank (Major)")
  )
  
  message(paste("\nFound", nrow(ny_fusion_check)/2, "elections where fusion voting changed the outcome"))
} else {
  message("No elections found where fusion voting changed the outcome in our dataset")
}


Analysis: The fusion voting system demonstrates how minor parties (Working Families Party, Conservative Party) can influence outcomes. In close races, securing multiple party line nominations can be decisive. This illustrates that electoral system design choices—even seemingly minor ones—can meaningfully affect results.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
3. Presidential Coattails
The “coattail effect” refers to the tendency of popular presidential candidates to boost their party’s congressional candidates. Analyzing this helps us understand whether presidential and congressional elections are becoming more or less coupled over time.


Show code
# Only analyze presidential election years present in both datasets
common_years <- intersect(unique(house_data$year), unique(pres_data$year))
pres_years <- common_years[common_years %% 4 == 0]

# Aggregate House votes by party/state/year
house_agg <- house_data %>%
  filter(year %in% pres_years) %>%
  group_by(year, state, party) %>%
  summarize(house_votes = sum(candidatevotes, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(year, state) %>%
  mutate(house_pct = house_votes / sum(house_votes)) %>%
  filter(party %in% c("DEMOCRAT", "REPUBLICAN"))

# Aggregate Presidential votes
pres_agg <- pres_data %>%
  filter(year %in% pres_years) %>%
  group_by(year, state, party_simplified) %>%
  summarize(pres_votes = sum(candidatevotes, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(year, state) %>%
  mutate(pres_pct = pres_votes / sum(pres_votes)) %>%
  rename(party = party_simplified) %>%
  mutate(party = str_to_upper(party)) %>%
  filter(party %in% c("DEMOCRAT", "REPUBLICAN"))

# Join and Compare
comparison <- house_agg %>%
  inner_join(pres_agg, by = c("year", "state", "party")) %>%
  mutate(difference = pres_pct - house_pct)

# Summary statistics by year and party
coattail_summary <- comparison %>%
  group_by(year, party) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_diff = mean(difference, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_diff = median(difference, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Visualization
ggplot(comparison, aes(x = year, y = difference, color = party)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.3, width = 0.3, size = 1) + 
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("DEMOCRAT" = "#2E74C0", "REPUBLICAN" = "#CB454A")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Presidential Coattails: Do Presidential Candidates Outperform House Candidates?",
    subtitle = "Positive values indicate presidential candidate received higher vote share than House candidates",
    y = "Presidential Vote % - House Vote %",
    x = "Election Year",
    color = "Party",
    caption = "Each point represents one state. Smoothed trend lines show LOESS fit."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, face = "italic")
  )









Show code
# Summary table
kable(
  coattail_summary %>% 
    pivot_wider(names_from = party, values_from = c(mean_diff, median_diff)) %>%
    arrange(year),
  caption = "Presidential Coattail Effect by Year (Mean and Median Difference)",
  digits = 4
)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
Task 5: 2000 Electoral College Map
The 2000 election (Bush v. Gore) was one of the most controversial in US history, decided by just 537 votes in Florida and resulting in a Supreme Court case. This map visualizes the state-by-state results.


Show code
# Get 2000 election results
election_2000 <- pres_data %>%
  filter(year == 2000) %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  filter(candidatevotes == max(candidatevotes)) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(state, winner = party_simplified, candidate, candidatevotes) %>%
  mutate(
    region = tolower(state),
    winner = str_to_upper(winner)
  )

# Calculate EC votes for 2000 (using House seats from 2000)
ec_2000 <- house_data %>%
  filter(year == 2000) %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  summarize(ec_votes = n_distinct(district) + 2, .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(region = tolower(state))

# Add DC (3 EC votes, 2 Gore, 1 Faithless/Abstained)
# Note: Data typically records just candidate votes. We manually handle DC to match historical record (266 Gore).
dc_2000 <- tibble(
  state = "DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA",
  winner = "DEMOCRAT",
  candidate = "AL GORE",
  candidatevotes = 171923,
  region = "district of columbia"
)
election_2000 <- bind_rows(election_2000, dc_2000)

ec_dc <- tibble(state = "DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA", ec_votes = 3, region = "district of columbia")
ec_2000 <- bind_rows(ec_2000, ec_dc)

# Join with map data (Using Built-in Maps for Reliability)
us_map_data <- map_data("state")
map_plot_data <- us_map_data %>%
  left_join(election_2000, by = "region") %>%
  left_join(ec_2000, by = "region")

# Create state centroids for labels
state_centers <- map_plot_data %>%
  group_by(region, winner, ec_votes) %>%
  summarize(
    long = mean(long),
    lat = mean(lat),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(winner))

# Plot
ggplot(map_plot_data, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = winner)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(
    data = state_centers,
    aes(x = long, y = lat, label = ec_votes, group = NULL),
    color = "white",
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_fixed(1.3) + 
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("DEMOCRAT" = "#2E74C0", "REPUBLICAN" = "#CB454A", "GREEN" = "#00C000"),
    name = "Winner",
    na.value = "gray90"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "2000 Presidential Election Results",
    subtitle = "Bush (271 EC votes) defeated Gore (266 EC votes) despite losing popular vote by 543,895 votes",
    caption = "Numbers show Electoral College votes per state"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", size = 9)
  )









Show code
# Verify EC totals
ec_totals_2000 <- election_2000 %>%
  left_join(ec_2000, by = "region") %>%
  group_by(winner) %>%
  summarize(total_ec = sum(ec_votes, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

kable(
  ec_totals_2000,
  caption = "2000 Electoral College Vote Totals",
  col.names = c("Party", "Electoral Votes")
)



2000 Electoral College Vote Totals


Party
Electoral Votes




DEMOCRAT

Historical Context: This election highlighted the Electoral College’s ability to produce winners who lost the popular vote. Gore won 48.4% of the popular vote to Bush’s 47.9%, but Bush secured 271 EC votes to Gore’s 266 (one DC elector abstained in protest). The Florida recount controversy led to the Supreme Court case Bush v. Gore.

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
section: Task 7: The Fact Check - Electoral System Comparison
text:
Task 7: The Fact Check - Electoral System Comparison
Now I calculate the winner of every election under four different rules:

Winner-Take-All (History): The actual result.
District + 2: Winner of each district gets 1 vote; State winner gets 2.
Proportional (State): EC votes divided by state popular vote %.
Direct Popular Vote: National popular vote winner.



Show code
# --- Helper: Robust Party Name Cleaning ---
clean_party <- function(x) {
  str_to_upper(str_trim(as.character(x)))
}

# --- Main Calculation Function ---
calculate_ec_scenarios <- function() {
  results <- list()
  
  # Identify valid Presidential Election Years (divisible by 4) present in BOTH datasets
  common_years <- intersect(unique(house_data$year), unique(pres_data$year))
  pres_years <- sort(common_years[common_years %% 4 == 0])
  
  message("\n=== Calculating Electoral College Scenarios ===")
  message(paste("Analyzing years:", paste(pres_years, collapse = ", ")))
  
  for(yr in pres_years) {
    
    # Filter & Clean Data for the Year
    p_yr <- pres_data %>% 
      filter(year == yr) %>%
      mutate(party = clean_party(party_simplified))
      
    h_yr <- house_data %>% 
      filter(year == yr) %>%
      mutate(party = clean_party(party))
    
    # Calculate EC Votes per State (Districts + 2 Senate seats)
    ec_alloc <- h_yr %>% 
      group_by(state) %>% 
      summarize(ec_votes = n_distinct(district) + 2, .groups = "drop")
    
    # Add DC (3 EC votes)
    ec_alloc <- bind_rows(
      ec_alloc,
      tibble(state = "DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA", ec_votes = 3)
    )
    
    # --- Scenario 1: Actual Winner-Take-All (History) ---
    wta_winner <- p_yr %>%
      group_by(state) %>%
      filter(candidatevotes == max(candidatevotes)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      left_join(ec_alloc, by = "state") %>%
      group_by(party) %>%
      summarize(ec_total = sum(ec_votes, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
      filter(ec_total == max(ec_total)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      mutate(scenario = "Winner-Take-All", year = yr)
    
    # --- Scenario 2: District + 2 ---
    # **IMPORTANT ASSUMPTION**: We use House election results as a proxy for 
    # presidential district-level results.
    
    # Part A: District Winners (1 EC Vote each)
    dist_winners <- h_yr %>%
      group_by(state, district) %>%
      filter(candidatevotes == max(candidatevotes)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      count(party, name = "dist_ec")
    
    # Part B: State Winners (2 EC Votes each) - use presidential data
    state_winners <- p_yr %>%
      group_by(state) %>%
      filter(candidatevotes == max(candidatevotes)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      ungroup() %>%
      count(party, name = "state_wins") %>%
      mutate(state_ec = state_wins * 2) %>%
      select(party, state_ec)
    
    # Combine
    d2_winner <- full_join(dist_winners, state_winners, by = "party") %>%
      mutate(
        total_ec = replace_na(dist_ec, 0) + replace_na(state_ec, 0)
      ) %>%
      filter(total_ec == max(total_ec)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      transmute(party, ec_total = total_ec, scenario = "District + 2", year = yr)
    
    # --- Scenario 3: National Popular Vote ---
    pop_winner <- p_yr %>%
      group_by(party) %>%
      summarize(total_votes = sum(candidatevotes, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
      filter(total_votes == max(total_votes)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      mutate(
        scenario = "National Popular Vote", 
        year = yr, 
        ec_total = NA_real_
      ) %>%
      select(party, ec_total, scenario, year)
    
    # --- Scenario 4: Proportional (State) ---
    prop_winner <- p_yr %>%
      group_by(state) %>%
      mutate(vote_share = candidatevotes / sum(candidatevotes)) %>%
      left_join(ec_alloc, by = "state") %>%
      mutate(earned_ec = vote_share * ec_votes) %>%
      group_by(party) %>%
      summarize(ec_total = sum(earned_ec, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
      filter(ec_total == max(ec_total)) %>%
      slice(1) %>%
      mutate(scenario = "Proportional (State)", year = yr)
      
    # Store results
    year_results <- bind_rows(wta_winner, d2_winner, pop_winner, prop_winner)
    results[[as.character(yr)]] <- year_results
  }
  
  bind_rows(results)
}

# Run Calculation
scenarios <- calculate_ec_scenarios()



Identifying Divergent Elections
Elections where different systems would have produced different winners are particularly important for evaluating Electoral College bias.


Show code
# Format the Table for Display
divergences <- scenarios %>%
  select(year, scenario, party) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = scenario, values_from = party) %>%
  # Filter for years where the result is NOT the same across all methods
  filter(
    `Winner-Take-All` != `National Popular Vote` | 
    `Winner-Take-All` != `District + 2` | 
    `Winner-Take-All` != `Proportional (State)`
  ) %>%
  arrange(year)

kable(
  divergences,
  caption = "Elections with Divergent Outcomes Across Allocation Systems",
  col.names = c("Year", "Winner-Take-All", "District + 2", "National Popular Vote", "Proportional")
)

title: STA 9750 Mini-Project #03: The Electoral College
text:
Systematic Bias Analysis
To evaluate whether the Electoral College systematically favors one party, I’ll analyze the direction of bias across all elections:


Show code
bias_analysis <- scenarios %>%
  select(year, scenario, party) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = scenario, values_from = party) %>%
  mutate(
    wta_vs_popular = case_when(
      `Winner-Take-All` == `National Popular Vote` ~ "Same Winner",
      `Winner-Take-All` == "REPUBLICAN" & `National Popular Vote` == "DEMOCRAT" ~ "Favored Republican",
      `Winner-Take-All` == "DEMOCRAT" & `National Popular Vote` == "REPUBLICAN" ~ "Favored Democrat",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    d2_vs_popular = case_when(
      `District + 2` == `National Popular Vote` ~ "Same Winner",
      `District + 2` == "REPUBLICAN" & `National Popular Vote` == "DEMOCRAT" ~ "Favored Republican",
      `District + 2` == "DEMOCRAT" & `National Popular Vote` == "REPUBLICAN" ~ "Favored Democrat",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# Summary of bias
bias_summary <- bias_analysis %>%
  summarize(
    total_elections = n(),
    wta_same = sum(wta_vs_popular == "Same Winner"),
    wta_favored_rep = sum(wta_vs_popular == "Favored Republican"),
    wta_favored_dem = sum(wta_vs_popular == "Favored Democrat"),
    d2_same = sum(d2_vs_popular == "Same Winner"),
    d2_favored_rep = sum(d2_vs_popular == "Favored Republican"),
    d2_favored_dem = sum(d2_vs_popular == "Favored Democrat")
  )

title: CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis: A Monte Carlo Approach
text: This analysis evaluates CUNY’s two retirement plans: the defined-benefit Teachers Retirement System (TRS) and the defined-contribution Optional Retirement Plan (ORP). Using Monte Carlo simulation with bootstrap resampling of historical market data, we compare long-term financial outcomes to assist new faculty in making this permanent decision.

title: CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis: A Monte Carlo Approach
CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis: A Monte Carlo Approach
text:
Monte Carlo Simulation


Code
run_monte_carlo <- function(n_sims = 500, start_age = 30, ret_age = 65, 
                           death_age = 85, start_sal = 67784) {
  n_work <- (ret_age - start_age) * 12
  n_ret <- (death_age - ret_age) * 12
  
  map_dfr(1:n_sims, function(sim) {
    work_sample <- slice_sample(combined_monthly_data, n = n_work, replace = TRUE)
    ret_sample <- slice_sample(combined_monthly_data, n = n_ret, replace = TRUE)
    
    # Accumulation phase
    salary_hist <- tibble(month = 1:n_work) |>
      mutate(
        wage_growth = work_sample$wage_monthly_change,
        salary_monthly = (start_sal / 12) * cumprod(1 + wage_growth),
        salary_annual = salary_monthly * 12,
        age = start_age + (month / 12)
      )
    
    trs_monthly <- calculate_trs_benefit(salary_hist, n_work)
    
    # ORP accumulation
    orp_bal <- 0
    for (i in 1:n_work) {
      emp_rate <- get_contribution_rate(salary_hist$salary_annual[i])
      empr_rate <- if_else(i / 12 <= 7, 0.08, 0.10)
      contrib <- salary_hist$salary_monthly[i] * (emp_rate + empr_rate)
      
      alloc <- get_asset_allocation(salary_hist$age[i])
      ret <- alloc$us * work_sample$us_equity_return[i] +
             alloc$intl * work_sample$intl_equity_return[i] +
             alloc$bond * work_sample$bond_return[i] +
             alloc$short * work_sample$treasury_monthly_return[i]
      
      orp_bal <- orp_bal * (1 + ret) + contrib
    }
    
    # Decumulation phase
    trs_total <- 0
    orp_total <- 0
    curr_withdraw <- orp_bal * 0.04 / 12
    orp_curr <- orp_bal
    
    for (j in 1:n_ret) {
      if (j > 12 && (j - 1) %% 12 == 0) {
        annual_cpi <- prod(1 + ret_sample$cpi_monthly_change[(j - 12):(j - 1)]) - 1
        trs_monthly <- apply_trs_cola(trs_monthly, annual_cpi)
        curr_withdraw <- curr_withdraw * (1 + annual_cpi)
      }
      
      trs_total <- trs_total + trs_monthly
      
      if (orp_curr > 0) {
        amt <- min(curr_withdraw, orp_curr)
        orp_total <- orp_total + amt
        orp_curr <- orp_curr - amt
        
        alloc <- get_asset_allocation(ret_age + j / 12)
        ret <- alloc$us * ret_sample$us_equity_return[j] +
               alloc$intl * ret_sample$intl_equity_return[j] +
               alloc$bond * ret_sample$bond_return[j] +
               alloc$short * ret_sample$treasury_monthly_return[j]
        
        orp_curr <- max(0, orp_curr * (1 + ret))
      }
    }
    
    tibble(
      sim = sim,
      trs_total = trs_total,
      orp_total = orp_total,
      orp_better = orp_total > trs_total,
      depleted = orp_curr == 0,
      final_balance = orp_curr
    )
  })
}

sim_results <- run_monte_carlo()

title: CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis: A Monte Carlo Approach
text:
Recommendation
Based on the Monte Carlo simulation, TRS is recommended for faculty starting at age 30 with moderate risk tolerance.
Key Trade-offs:

TRS: Provides predictable, inflation-adjusted income around $3,500/month, eliminating longevity risk
ORP: Offers upside potential (some scenarios exceed $12,000/month), but most likely outcome ($2,000-$3,000/month) is lower than TRS

For risk-neutral or risk-averse individuals, TRS offers a superior income floor. ORP is only preferable if willing to accept >50% chance of underperformance in exchange for small probability of significant wealth.

title: CUNY Retirement Plan Analysis: A Monte Carlo Approach
text:
Sensitivity Analysis


Code
run_sim_for_age <- function(ret_age_input, n_sims = 200) {
  start_age <- 30
  death_age <- 85
  start_sal <- 67784
  
  n_work <- (ret_age_input - start_age) * 12
  n_ret <- (death_age - ret_age_input) * 12
  
  results <- map_dfr(1:n_sims, function(sim) {
    work_sample <- slice_sample(combined_monthly_data, n = n_work, replace = TRUE)
    ret_sample <- slice_sample(combined_monthly_data, n = n_ret, replace = TRUE)
    
    salary_hist <- tibble(month = 1:n_work) |>
      mutate(
        wage_growth = work_sample$wage_monthly_change,
        salary_monthly = (start_sal / 12) * cumprod(1 + wage_growth),
        salary_annual = salary_monthly * 12,
        age = start_age + (month / 12)
      )
    
    trs_monthly <- calculate_trs_benefit(salary_hist, n_work)
    
    orp_bal <- 0
    for (i in 1:n_work) {
      emp_rate <- get_contribution_rate(salary_hist$salary_annual[i])
      er_rate <- if (i / 12 <= 7) 0.08 else 0.10
      contrib <- salary_hist$salary_monthly[i] * (emp_rate + er_rate)
      
      alloc <- get_asset_allocation(salary_hist$age[i])
      ret <- alloc$us * work_sample$us_equity_return[i] +
             alloc$intl * work_sample$intl_equity_return[i] +
             alloc$bond * work_sample$bond_return[i] +
             alloc$short * work_sample$treasury_monthly_return[i]
      
      orp_bal <- orp_bal * (1 + ret) + contrib
    }
    
    trs_lifetime <- trs_monthly * n_ret
    orp_lifetime <- (orp_bal * 0.04) * (n_ret / 12)
    
    tibble(orp_better = orp_lifetime > trs_lifetime)
  })
  
  tibble(
    Retirement_Age = ret_age_input,
    ORP_Win_Rate = mean(results$orp_better)
  )
}

sensitivity_results <- bind_rows(
  run_sim_for_age(55),
  run_sim_for_age(60),
  run_sim_for_age(65),
  run_sim_for_age(70)
)

p <- ggplot(sensitivity_results, aes(x = Retirement_Age, y = ORP_Win_Rate)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#e67e22", size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  labs(
    title = "Sensitivity Analysis: ORP Win Rate vs. Retirement Age",
    subtitle = "Does working longer favor the investment-based plan?",
    x = "Retirement Age",
    y = "Probability ORP Exceeds TRS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p)






Analysis: Early retirement (age 55) strongly favors TRS with ORP winning <30% of the time. As retirement age increases to 70, ORP’s probability of outperforming approaches 50%. Longer careers allow more time for compound growth and market recovery, making ORP more attractive for faculty planning to work into their 70s.
